evals.name: eval-capture-clear
evals.prompt:
During a learning session about RAG systems, this conversation happened:

User: 'I keep seeing HNSW mentioned for vector search but I don't get why approximate search is acceptable. If I'm doing semantic search, don't I want exact results?'

Claude: 'Exact nearest neighbor search guarantees you find the mathematically closest vector. HNSW is approximate — it misses some neighbors. But in semantic space, vectors that are close to your query vector are semantically similar enough to be useful. The #1 true nearest neighbor and the #3 approximate nearest neighbor are probably both relevant to your query. The small accuracy loss is irrelevant for the use case.'

User: 'Wait so you're saying the error tolerance of HNSW matches the problem tolerance of semantic search?'

Claude: 'Exactly. And HNSW is orders of magnitude faster — at 1M vectors, exact search takes seconds, HNSW takes milliseconds. For a real RAG system, that latency difference matters more than whether you got the #1 vs #2 nearest neighbor.'

User: 'Oh that's actually kind of beautiful. The approximation is fine because semantic meaning is fuzzy anyway.'

/capture-insight why HNSW approximate search is acceptable for semantic retrieval
evals.expected_output: A markdown file created at ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/ai_engineering/ with today's date in the filename, all required sections present, status=raw, under 400 words, INDEX.md updated.
expectations: A .md file was created inside ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/ai_engineering/ with '2026-03-19' in the filename
The file contains a '## The Struggle' section with non-empty content
The file contains a '## The Drill-Down' section with non-empty content
The file contains a '## The Aha Moment' section with non-empty content
The file contains a '## The One-Liner' section with a blockquote (line starting with '>')
The file metadata contains 'Status: raw'
The file body is under 400 words
~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/INDEX.md exists and contains a row referencing the new file
The One-Liner or Struggle sections preserve the user's casual language — the user said 'beautiful' and 'fuzzy', this energy should appear rather than being sanitized into formal prose
The content does not fabricate struggles or technical details not present in the provided conversation

evals.name: eval-capture-casual
evals.prompt: I just had a really good insight while building my RAG pipeline. I realized that chunking strategy matters more than the embedding model for retrieval quality on domain-specific corpora. I spent two days swapping embedding models and barely moved the needle. Then I fixed my chunk boundaries to respect paragraph structure and my RAGAS scores jumped 28%. This is a good insight — save it as a learning insight.
evals.expected_output: A markdown file created in ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/ even without a slash command. All required sections present, status=raw, content preserves the '28%' detail and user's casual voice.
expectations: A .md file was created in ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/ — confirming the skill triggered without an explicit /capture-insight slash command
The file contains all four sections: ## The Struggle, ## The Drill-Down, ## The Aha Moment, ## The One-Liner
The file metadata contains 'Status: raw'
The file content references '28%' — this specific detail from the prompt must be preserved
The Struggle and Drill-Down sections use the user's phrasing — 'barely moved the needle' should appear rather than a formal paraphrase
The Aha Moment correctly captures that chunk boundaries (not model size) were the root cause of retrieval failures

evals.name: eval-capture-no-intent
evals.expected_output: No file created. The skill asks for clarification since no intent was provided.
expectations: No new .md file was created in ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/
The response asks the user to clarify what they want to capture, not an error message and not a fabricated insight
The clarification response points to the correct usage format (e.g., '/capture-insight <what to capture>')

evals.name: eval-capture-should-not-trigger
evals.prompt: Can you save this markdown file to disk? I want to keep a record of our conversation. Just save the current conversation as a .md file in my project directory.
evals.expected_output: Skill does NOT trigger. No file created in ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/. Request handled as a plain file-save.
expectations: No file was created in ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/
~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/INDEX.md was not modified
The agent handled the request as a plain file-save without routing it through the learning-insights capture workflow

evals.name: eval-polish-request
evals.prompt: I want to turn this insight into a LinkedIn post: chunking strategy matters more than embedding model choice for RAG quality. I tested both extensively — swapping from MiniLM to a larger model barely moved my RAGAS scores, but fixing chunk boundaries to respect paragraph structure gave me a 28% jump. The lesson: your retrieval pipeline is only as good as your chunks. Create a LinkedIn post from this.
evals.expected_output: Skill triggers. LinkedIn post variations produced. Posts are under 3000 chars, have strong hooks, no clichés, sound human.
expectations: The skill triggered and produced LinkedIn post content
Each post variation is under 3000 characters
The posts do not contain banned patterns: 'Here's the thing', 'Game-changer', 'Stop.', 'Read that again', excessive hashtags, or one-word sentence hooks
The first 1-2 lines of each post create genuine curiosity through a surprising or counterintuitive claim
The posts include concrete details (28%, RAGAS, MiniLM) rather than generic motivational language
The posts sound like a practitioner sharing a real learning, not a thought leader performing expertise
